pending.body:
finding_summary: `src/adapters/fs.rs:235-238` drops `ProcessStartPrecision` on the floor, and `legacy_pid_record_live_state()` (`src/adapters/fs.rs:820-845`) treats any live process whose derived start time is `<= record.started_at` as definitively `Live`. On macOS/BSD, `process_started_at()` only has whole-second precision (`src/adapters/fs.rs:323-328`), so a PID reused later in the same second as the original `run.pid` write is still accepted as the original process. That means legacy pid files can still be misclassified as live after PID reuse on the non-Linux platforms this bead is supposed to harden, causing resume/recovery/cleanup to wait on an unrelated process.
severity: medium
affected_files: ["src/adapters/fs.rs"]
remediation_hint: Make the coarse-grained path conservative. When the live start time is only known to whole-second precision, same-second comparisons should return `RunningUnverified` (or equivalent) rather than `Live`; only timestamps at least one full second after the rounded start should be treated as a definite match.

Test `legacy_pid_liveness_marks_running_process_without_start_time_as_unverified` at src/adapters/fs.rs:5934 is missing a `#[cfg(unix)]` gate. The test creates a legacy pid record using `std::process::id()` and expects `RunningUnverified`, but on non-unix platforms `is_pid_running_unchecked` unconditionally returns `false`, so `legacy_pid_record_live_state` would return `Stale` instead, causing the assertion to fail. All peer tests that rely on the current process being detectable as running (e.g., lines 5917, 5953, 5973, 7420) are gated by `#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]` or `#[cfg(unix)]`. Add `#[cfg(unix)]` before this test to match.

status_summary: running: final review restart round 4 -> Plan and Implement
